message:
feat(ftui): enforce one-writer output routing for TUI runtime (FTUI-03.2)

Add output gate module that tracks TUI session state via a process-global
atomic flag. When the TUI rendering pipeline owns the terminal, stray
writes to stdout/stderr are suppressed to prevent cursor/layout corruption
per FrankenTUI's one-writer rule.

- output_gate.rs: GatePhase enum (Inactive/Active/Suspended), TuiAwareWriter
  for tracing, is_output_suppressed() check function
- terminal_session.rs: SessionGuard toggles gate on enter/leave/suspend/resume;
  CrosstermSession suspend/resume also toggle for command handoff
- crash.rs: panic hook checks gate before eprintln! (crash bundle still
  written to disk regardless)
- logging.rs: TuiAwareWriter replaces raw stderr in tracing subscriber when
  tui/ftui feature is active

8 new tests (6 output_gate, 2 terminal_session integration).
Closes wa-3cso.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-1m2","title":"Explanation templates: reusable reason patterns for wa why and errors","description":"# Explanation Templates: Reusable reason patterns for wa why

## Purpose
Provide consistent, helpful explanations for common scenarios via template system.

## Implementation

### Template Structure
```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct ExplanationTemplate {
    pub id: &'static str,
    pub scenario: &'static str,
    pub brief: &'static str,
    pub detailed: &'static str,
    pub suggestions: Vec<&'static str>,
    pub see_also: Vec<&'static str>,
}
```

### Built-in Templates

#### Policy Denials
```rust
pub static DENY_ALT_SCREEN: ExplanationTemplate = ExplanationTemplate {
    id: "deny.alt_screen",
    scenario: "Send denied because alt-screen is active",
    brief: "Pane is in full-screen mode (vim, less, etc.)",
    detailed: r#"
The pane is currently displaying an alternate screen buffer, which typically
means a full-screen application like vim, less, htop, or similar is running.

Sending text while alt-screen is active could:
- Corrupt the application state
- Cause unintended keystrokes
- Interfere with user interaction

The safety policy blocks sends to alt-screen panes by default.
"#,
    suggestions: vec![
        "Exit the full-screen application first",
        "Use --force if you're certain this is safe",
        "Configure policy to allow specific alt-screen apps",
    ],
    see_also: vec!["wa policy", "wa status --pane <id>"],
};

pub static DENY_COMMAND_RUNNING: ExplanationTemplate = ExplanationTemplate {
    id: "deny.command_running",
    scenario: "Send denied because a command is running",
    brief: "Another command is currently executing in the pane",
    detailed: r#"
The pane has an active command running (detected via OSC 133 markers or
heuristics). Sending text while a command runs could:

- Interrupt the running command
- Queue input for later (confusing)
- Cause the shell to misinterpret input

wa waits for command completion before sending unless overridden.
"#,
    suggestions: vec![
        "Wait for the current command to finish",
        "Use Ctrl-C to cancel the running command first",
        "Use --wait-for to send after a specific pattern",
    ],
    see_also: vec!["wa status", "wa send --wait-for"],
};

pub static DENY_RECENT_GAP: ExplanationTemplate = ExplanationTemplate {
    id: "deny.recent_gap",
    scenario: "Send denied due to recent output gap",
    brief: "Pane had no output recently, possibly waiting for input",
    detailed: r#"
wa detected a gap in pane output that suggests the pane might be:
- Waiting for user input at a prompt
- Displaying a confirmation dialog
- In an unknown state

The policy requires a prompt marker (OSC 133) or manual confirmation.
"#,
    suggestions: vec![
        "Check the pane manually to see its state",
        "Use --force if you've verified the pane is ready",
        "Enable OSC 133 support in your shell for better detection",
    ],
    see_also: vec!["wa capabilities --pane <id>"],
};
```

#### Workflow Explanations
```rust
pub static WORKFLOW_USAGE_LIMIT: ExplanationTemplate = ExplanationTemplate {
    id: "workflow.usage_limit",
    scenario: "Why handle_usage_limits workflow was triggered",
    brief: "Codex hit its daily token usage limit",
    detailed: r#"
The Codex agent reported it has reached its usage limit. This typically
happens when:

- Daily token quota exceeded
- Account-level rate limiting triggered

The handle_usage_limits workflow will:
1. Gracefully exit the current Codex session
2. Parse the session summary for resume ID
3. Select an alternate OpenAI account
4. Complete device auth flow
5. Resume the session with new credentials
"#,
    suggestions: vec![
        "Let the workflow complete automatically",
        "Check account status with: caut status",
        "Configure account pool in wa.toml",
    ],
    see_also: vec!["wa workflow status", "caut"],
};
```

### Template Registry
```rust
lazy_static! {
    pub static ref EXPLANATION_TEMPLATES: HashMap<&'static str, &'static ExplanationTemplate> = {
        let mut m = HashMap::new();
        m.insert("deny.alt_screen", &DENY_ALT_SCREEN);
        m.insert("deny.command_running", &DENY_COMMAND_RUNNING);
        m.insert("deny.recent_gap", &DENY_RECENT_GAP);
        m.insert("workflow.usage_limit", &WORKFLOW_USAGE_LIMIT);
        // ... more templates
        m
    };
}

pub fn get_explanation(id: &str) -> Option<&'static ExplanationTemplate> {
    EXPLANATION_TEMPLATES.get(id).copied()
}
```

### Template Interpolation
```rust
pub fn render_explanation(
    template: &ExplanationTemplate,
    context: &HashMap<String, String>,
) -> String {
    let mut output = template.detailed.to_string();
    for (key, value) in context {
        output = output.replace(&format!("{{{}}}", key), value);
    }
    output
}
```

## Testing
- All templates have valid structure
- Interpolation works correctly
- No broken cross-references

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Templates for all common denial reasons
- [ ] Templates for all workflow triggers
- [ ] wa why uses templates consistently
- [ ] Templates documented in help

 {"id":"wa-2ji","title":"Stress test: scalability with 50+ panes and large transcripts","description":"# Task: Scalability Stress Tests

## Goal
Validate wa performance and stability under high load conditions.

## Test Scenarios

### 1. Many Panes (50+)
```bash
# Setup: Create 50 panes with concurrent activity
for i in {1..50}; do
    wezterm cli spawn --new-window -- bash -c "while true; do echo line $i; sleep 0.1; done" &
done

# Run watcher and measure:
wa watch &
sleep 60

# Metrics to collect:
# - CPU usage (should stay < 50%)
# - Memory usage (should stay < 500MB)
# - Ingest lag (should stay < 100ms)
# - Queue depth (should not grow unbounded)
```

### 2. Large Transcript (1M+ lines)
```bash
# Generate large output in single pane
for i in {1..1000000}; do
    echo "Log line $i with some content to match patterns against"
done

# Verify:
# - FTS indexing completes
# - Search remains responsive (< 500ms for broad query)
# - Memory usage bounded
# - No disk space explosion
```

### 3. High Event Rate
```bash
# Trigger many pattern matches rapidly
for i in {1..1000}; do
    echo "ERROR: test error $i"
    sleep 0.01
done

# Verify:
# - Event deduplication works
# - No event queue overflow
# - Notifications throttled appropriately
```

### 4. Long-Running Stability (24h)
```bash
# Run watcher for extended period with normal activity
wa watch &
# ... normal usage for 24 hours ...

# Check for:
# - Memory leaks (memory growth over time)
# - File handle leaks
# - Database size growth rate
# - No crashes or hangs
```

## Metrics Collection
```rust
struct StressTestMetrics {
    cpu_usage_percent: Vec<f32>,
    memory_mb: Vec<f32>,
    ingest_lag_ms: Vec<f32>,
    queue_depth: Vec<usize>,
    events_per_second: f32,
    fts_query_p99_ms: f32,
}
```

## Pass Criteria
| Scenario | Metric | Budget |
|----------|--------|--------|
| 50 panes | CPU idle | < 50% |
| 50 panes | Memory | < 500MB |
| 50 panes | Ingest lag | < 100ms |
| 1M lines | FTS query | < 500ms |
| 1M lines | Memory | < 1GB |
| 24h run | Memory growth | < 10% |
| 24h run | Crashes | 0 |

## Testing
- CI integration: run scaled-down version (10 panes, 100k lines)
- Manual: full stress test before releases
- Artifacts: metrics graphs, memory profiles

## Acceptance Criteria
- Stress test suite exists and is runnable
- Budget thresholds are enforced
- Regressions are visible in CI
- Long-running test detects leaks

 {"id":"wa-2sfj","title":"FTUI-05.5.a Triage ranking and action parity checklist","description":"## Background
Triage behavior directly affects operator prioritization; ranking/action semantics must stay stable after migration.

## Deliverables
- parity checklist for ranking order, tie-breaking, action affordances, and state transitions
- deterministic test scenarios for changing queue composition and repeated refreshes
- evidence references to logs/snapshots demonstrating decision transparency

## Acceptance Criteria

 {"id":"wa-2tk","title":"Metrics export: Prometheus endpoint with counters, gauges, histograms","description":"# Metrics Export

## Purpose
Expose Prometheus-compatible metrics for monitoring and alerting.

## Implementation

### Metrics Registry
```rust
use prometheus::{Registry, Counter, CounterVec, Gauge, GaugeVec, Histogram, HistogramVec};

lazy_static\! {
    pub static ref REGISTRY: Registry = Registry::new();
    
    // Counters
    pub static ref EVENTS_DETECTED: CounterVec = register_counter_vec\!(
        "wa_events_detected_total",
        "Total events detected",
        &["type", "agent"]
    ).unwrap();
    
    pub static ref WORKFLOWS_COMPLETED: CounterVec = register_counter_vec\!(
        "wa_workflows_completed_total",
        "Total workflows completed",
        &["workflow", "status"]
    ).unwrap();
    
    pub static ref POLICY_DECISIONS: CounterVec = register_counter_vec\!(
        "wa_policy_decisions_total",
        "Total policy decisions",
        &["decision", "action"]
    ).unwrap();
    
    // Gauges
    pub static ref PANES_OBSERVED: Gauge = register_gauge\!(
        "wa_panes_observed",
        "Number of observed panes"
    ).unwrap();
    
    pub static ref QUEUE_DEPTH: Gauge = register_gauge\!(
        "wa_queue_depth",
        "Current event queue depth"
    ).unwrap();
    
    // Histograms
    pub static ref PATTERN_MATCH_DURATION: Histogram = register_histogram\!(
        "wa_pattern_match_duration_seconds",
        "Time to run pattern matching",
        vec\![0.0001, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1]
    ).unwrap();
}
```

### HTTP Endpoint
```rust
use axum::{Router, routing::get};
use prometheus::Encoder;

async fn metrics_handler() -> impl IntoResponse {
    let encoder = prometheus::TextEncoder::new();
    let metric_families = REGISTRY.gather();
    let mut buffer = Vec::new();
    encoder.encode(&metric_families, &mut buffer).unwrap();
    (
        [(header::CONTENT_TYPE, "text/plain; charset=utf-8")],
        buffer,
    )
}

pub fn metrics_router() -> Router {
    Router::new().route("/metrics", get(metrics_handler))
}
```

### Metric Instrumentation Points
- Event detection: increment counter, record duration
- Workflow execution: increment counter by status
- Policy decisions: increment by decision type
- Pane changes: update gauge
- Pattern matching: record histogram

## Metrics Catalog

| Metric | Type | Labels | Description |
|--------|------|--------|-------------|
| wa_events_detected_total | counter | type, agent | Events detected |
| wa_workflows_completed_total | counter | workflow, status | Workflow completions |
| wa_policy_decisions_total | counter | decision, action | Policy evaluations |
| wa_panes_observed | gauge | - | Active panes |
| wa_queue_depth | gauge | - | Pending events |
| wa_db_size_bytes | gauge | - | Database size |
| wa_pattern_match_duration_seconds | histogram | - | Pattern match time |
| wa_workflow_step_duration_seconds | histogram | workflow, step | Step execution time |

## Testing
- Unit: Metrics increment correctly
- Integration: Endpoint serves valid Prometheus format
- E2E: Metrics change during workflow execution

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All core operations instrumented
- [ ] /metrics endpoint serves Prometheus format
- [ ] Cardinality kept reasonable (bounded labels)
- [ ] Histograms have sensible buckets
- [ ] Feature-flagged for minimal deployments

 {"id":"wa-2wed","title":"[EPIC] FrankenTUI Adoption Program for wa (across-the-board migration)","description":"## Background
`wa` currently has a feature-gated TUI built around `ratatui`/`crossterm` (`crates/wa-core/src/tui`). We now want to standardize on `/dp/frankentui` (ftui) across the project’s interactive terminal surfaces.

FrankenTUI contributes a stricter kernel model:
- one-writer rule for terminal ownership
- deterministic render loop (buffer/diff/presenter)
- inline-first behavior for scrollback safety
- explicit operational quality gates (PTY E2E, snapshot and perf baselines)

## Program Goal
Migrate wa’s TUI architecture and operator workflows to ftui across the board, with a staged rollout that preserves behavior parity, improves determinism, and removes ratatui-centric technical debt.

## Scope
- dependency/build integration with `/dp/frankentui`
- runtime/output ownership model migration
- screen-by-screen UI migration
- command/input parity migration
- testing/perf/compatibility gates
- documentation, rollout, and old-stack decommissioning plan

## Non-Goals
- No broad rewrite of robot/MCP/storage logic unrelated to UI migration.
- No speculative feature expansion before parity and reliability gates are met.

## Success Criteria
- wa TUI experience runs on ftui with feature parity (or explicit intentional deltas).
- deterministic tests and PTY E2E prove stability under sustained output and resize/input stress.
- migration docs are self-contained enough that future contributors do not need to read old planning docs.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:03.398731926Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-08T21:32:43.602721861Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ftui","migration","program","tui"],"comments":[{"id":267,"issue_id":"wa-2wed","author":"GrayHarbor","text":"### FTUI Planning Notes (Self-Contained)

 {"id":"wa-dug.7","title":"Environment detection tests: all detection types, edge cases, E2E","description":"# Environment detection tests (unit + integration + E2E)

## Purpose
Comprehensive, deterministic test coverage for the environment detection system:
- detect WezTerm presence/version/socket
- detect shell + OSC 133 integration state
- detect agent types (from pane metadata/output)
- auto-configuration mapping (detections → optimal wa config)
- E2E integration via the standard harness/runner/registry

This bead is intentionally detailed because environment detection drives first-run UX (`wa doctor`, `wa setup`, and “it just works” defaults).

## Test categories

### 1) WezTerm detection tests (unit)
Use a mock environment / command runner abstraction.
```rust
#[test]
fn detects_wezterm_version() {
    let env = MockEnv::new()
        .with_command("wezterm", "--version", "wezterm 20260101-abc123");

    let info = WeztermInfo::detect_with_env(&env);
    assert_eq!(info.version, Some("20260101-abc123".into()));
}

#[test]
fn handles_missing_wezterm() {
    let env = MockEnv::new().without_command("wezterm");

    let info = WeztermInfo::detect_with_env(&env);
    assert!(info.version.is_none());
    assert!(!info.is_running);
}
```

### 2) Shell detection tests (unit)
Detect shell type and whether OSC 133 integration appears installed.
```rust
#[test]
fn detects_bash() {
    let env = MockEnv::new().with_env("SHELL", "/bin/bash");

    let info = ShellInfo::detect_with_env(&env);
    assert_eq!(info.shell_type, ShellType::Bash);
}

#[test]
fn detects_osc_133_in_zshrc() {
    let env = MockEnv::new()
        .with_env("SHELL", "/bin/zsh")
        .with_file_content("~/.zshrc", "precmd() { print -Pn \"\\e]133;A\\a\" }");

    let info = ShellInfo::detect_with_env(&env);
    assert!(info.osc_133_enabled);
}
```

### 3) Agent detection tests (unit)
```rust
#[tokio::test]
async fn detects_codex_from_title() {
    let panes = vec![mock_pane(0, "Codex - Project")];

    let agents = detect_agents(&panes).await;
    assert_eq!(agents.len(), 1);
    assert_eq!(agents[0].agent_type, AgentType::Codex);
}

#[tokio::test]
async fn no_false_positives_on_empty_pane() {
    let panes = vec![mock_pane(0, "zsh")];

    let agents = detect_agents(&panes).await;
    assert!(agents.is_empty());
}
```

### 4) Auto-configuration tests (unit/integration)
Validate the mapping layer (detections → config):
- polling interval adapts under load (bounded)
- safety strict in “production-ish” environments
- pattern packs match detected agents

Key constraint: deterministic tests (no wall-clock dependence).

### 5) E2E test (standard harness)
The E2E goal is to validate the *full integration* of detection into user surfaces, deterministically:
- `wa doctor` reports the expected environment snapshot
- auto-config output is valid and matches expectations
- `wa setup --dry-run` can consume detections and produce a stable plan

**Hard rule:** E2E must not depend on the host’s real state (no `pgrep wezterm`, no reading the user’s actual `~/.zshrc`, no ambient sockets).

#### Approach (hermetic stubbed environment)
Within the E2E harness workspace:
- Create a temp `HOME` with controlled shell rc files.
- Provide a fake `wezterm` binary in a temp dir and prepend it to `PATH`.
  - It must return deterministic outputs for `--version` and `cli list`.
- Run:
  - `wa doctor --format json`
  - `wa robot config --show-auto --format json` (or the canonical auto-config surface)
  - `wa setup --accept-defaults --dry-run`

#### Assertions
- Doctor JSON contains the expected detection fields (wezterm version, shell type, osc_133 enabled/disabled as configured by fixtures).
- Auto-config JSON contains required fields and respects detection-derived decisions.
- Setup dry-run output is deterministic and references only the E2E workspace paths.
- No secrets appear in stdout/stderr or artifacts.

#### Artifacts
- `doctor.json` (redacted)
- `auto_config.json`
- `setup_dry_run.txt`
- `env.txt` (PATH/HOME summary; redacted)
- `e2e.log` (timestamped steps + durations)

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Unit tests cover wezterm/shell/agent detection and avoid false positives.
- [ ] Auto-config tests cover all mapping rules and are deterministic.
- [ ] E2E case follows the standard harness contract and is hermetic (no host dependence).
- [ ] Artifacts/logging are detailed enough to debug failures without rerunning locally.

## Testing
- Unit: WezTerm/shell/agent detection with mock env and file fixtures.
- Integration: auto-config outputs validated for required fields.
- E2E: harness-based scenario with artifact capture (no ambient host probes).
- Determinism: all waits bounded; no fixed  {"id":"wa-dwa","title":"[EPIC] E2E Test Harness: Scripted scenarios, detailed logging, CI integration","description":"# [EPIC] E2E Test Harness

## Mission
Provide a **comprehensive E2E testing framework** with scripted scenarios, detailed logging, and CI integration that validates wa works correctly in real-world conditions.

## Why This Matters
Unit tests validate components in isolation. E2E tests validate **the whole system works together**:
- WezTerm integration actually works
- Patterns detect real agent output
- Workflows execute correctly end-to-end
- Error recovery handles real failures

Without E2E tests, we ship bugs that only manifest in production.

## E2E Test Architecture

### Test Harness
```
tests/e2e/
├── harness/
│   ├── mod.rs           # Test harness library
│   ├── fixtures.rs      # Test fixtures and setup
│   ├── assertions.rs    # Custom assertions
│   └── logging.rs       # Detailed test logging
├── scenarios/
│   ├── basic_ingest.rs  # Basic text capture
│   ├── pattern_detection.rs
│   ├── workflow_execution.rs
│   ├── policy_enforcement.rs
│   └── error_recovery.rs
├── scripts/
│   ├── run_all.sh       # Run all E2E tests
│   ├── setup_wezterm.sh # Setup test WezTerm instance
│   └── cleanup.sh       # Cleanup after tests
└── fixtures/
    ├── codex_session.txt
    ├── claude_session.txt
    └── error_scenarios/
```

Every E2E test produces detailed logs:

### Test Scenarios

#### 1. Basic Ingest
- Start wa watch
- Create mock pane with text
- Verify text captured in DB
- Verify FTS indexed

#### 2. Pattern Detection
- Inject known patterns into pane
- Verify events detected
- Verify event metadata correct
- Test false positive rejection

#### 3. Workflow Execution
- Trigger workflow via event
- Verify each step executes
- Verify final state correct
- Test workflow resume after restart

#### 4. Policy Enforcement
- Attempt blocked actions
- Verify policy denies correctly
- Test approval flow
- Verify audit trail

#### 5. Error Recovery
- Simulate WezTerm disconnect
- Verify graceful degradation
- Verify reconnection works
- Test circuit breaker behavior

### CI Integration
```yaml
# .github/workflows/e2e.yml
e2e-tests:
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  services:
    wezterm:
      image: wezterm/wezterm:latest
  steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - name: Build wa
      run: cargo build --release
    - name: Run E2E tests
      run: ./tests/e2e/scripts/run_all.sh
      env:
        E2E_LOG_LEVEL: debug
        E2E_TIMEOUT: 300
    - name: Upload logs
      if: always()
      uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
      with:
        name: e2e-logs
        path: tests/e2e/logs/
```

### Test Fixtures
Pre-recorded terminal sessions for reproducible testing:
```rust
struct TestFixture {
    name: &'static str,
    pane_title: &'static str,
    output_chunks: Vec<OutputChunk>,
    expected_events: Vec<ExpectedEvent>,
}

static CODEX_USAGE_LIMIT: TestFixture = TestFixture {
    name: "codex_usage_limit",
    pane_title: "codex @ /project",
    output_chunks: vec![
        OutputChunk { delay_ms: 0, text: "Working on task..." },
        OutputChunk { delay_ms: 5000, text: "\n\nYou've reached your usage limit.\n" },
        OutputChunk { delay_ms: 100, text: "Daily limit resets in 4 hours.\n" },
    ],
    expected_events: vec![
        ExpectedEvent { rule_id: "codex.usage_limit_reached", .. },
    ],
};
```

## Success Criteria
- E2E tests cover all major user journeys
- Tests produce detailed, actionable logs
- CI runs E2E tests on every PR
- Test failures are reproducible locally
- Test runtime < 5 minutes for full suite

## Testing
- Harness unit tests: fixture replay ordering, log writer, and custom assertions.
- Integration tests: scenario registry + harness lifecycle (setup/teardown).
- E2E: a minimal smoke scenario that exercises the harness itself and produces an artifact bundle.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Harness can run at least the five core scenarios with deterministic ordering.
- Each scenario emits a structured log with timestamps and a PASS/FAIL summary.
- CI runs the harness and uploads artifacts for every run.
- Failures are reproducible locally using the same fixture set.

 {"id":"wa-fno","title":"[EPIC] Plugin/Extension System: custom patterns, workflows, and integrations","description":"# [EPIC] Plugin/Extension System

## Mission
Enable users to extend wa with custom patterns, workflows, and integrations without modifying core code.

## Why This Matters
Every user has unique needs:
- Custom agent patterns not in built-in packs
- Proprietary workflow logic
- Integration with internal tools
- Organization-specific safety rules

A plugin system enables:
- **Customization** without forking
- **Sharing** of community extensions
- **Isolation** of custom code
- **Iteration** without core upgrades

## Core Insight: Extensibility as a Feature
The most successful developer tools are extensible (VS Code, Neovim, etc.). wa should be too.

## Scope

### Pattern Extensions
- User-defined pattern packs
- Custom extraction rules
- Pattern pack inheritance (extend built-in)

### Workflow Extensions
- Custom workflow definitions (YAML/TOML)
- Custom step implementations (Lua/WASM)
- Workflow hooks (pre/post)

### Integration Extensions
- Notification channels (Slack, Discord, custom)
- Export formatters
- Data sources

### Extension Management
- `wa ext install` / `wa ext list` / `wa ext remove`
- Local extensions directory
- Optional registry (community sharing)

## Success Criteria
- Users can add custom patterns without code changes
- Custom workflows run safely (sandboxed)
- Extensions are easy to share
- No security vulnerabilities from extensions

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests for extension loading
- Sandbox tests for untrusted code
- Integration tests for extension lifecycle
- Security tests for malicious extensions

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pattern packs loadable from user directory
- [ ] Custom workflows definable in YAML/TOML
- [ ] Extension management CLI works
- [ ] Extensions sandboxed (WASM or Lua)
- [ ] Documentation covers extension authoring
- [ ] Tests cover all extension types","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:54:32.094417025Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T17:54:32.094417025Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-extensibility","area-patterns","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-fno","depends_on_id":"wa-8bk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:33Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-fno.1","title":"User pattern packs: load custom rules from ~/.config/wa/patterns/","description":"# User pattern packs

## Purpose
Allow users to define custom pattern packs that load alongside built-in packs.

## User Pack Location
```
~/.config/wa/patterns/
├── my-org-patterns/
│   ├── pack.toml        # Pack metadata
│   └── rules.toml       # Rule definitions
└── custom-agent/
    ├── pack.toml
    └── rules.toml
```

## Pack Format
```toml
# pack.toml
[pack]
name = "my-org-patterns"
version = "1.0.0"
description = "Custom patterns for internal agents"
extends = "core.codex"  # Optional: inherit from built-in

# rules.toml
[[rules]]
id = "my-org.custom_warning"
name = "Custom Warning Pattern"
pattern = "\\[INTERNAL\\] Warning: (.+)"
severity = "warning"
extract = { message = "$1" }
agent_types = ["codex", "custom-agent"]
```

## Loading Priority
1. Built-in packs (highest priority)
2. User packs in order found
3. Workspace-local packs (.wa/patterns/)

For conflicts, later packs override earlier ones unless `extends` is used.

## Implementation
```rust
pub fn load_all_packs(config: &Config) -> Result<Vec<PatternPack>> {
    let mut packs = vec![];
    
    // Load built-in
    packs.extend(load_builtin_packs()?);
    
    // Load user packs
    let user_dir = config.patterns_dir();
    if user_dir.exists() {
        for entry in fs::read_dir(user_dir)? {
            let pack = PatternPack::load(entry?.path())?;
            packs.push(pack);
        }
    }
    
    // Load workspace packs
    if let Some(workspace_dir) = config.workspace_patterns_dir() {
        // Similar loading
    }
    
    // Resolve inheritance and conflicts
    resolve_pack_inheritance(&mut packs)?;
    
    Ok(packs)
}
```

## Validation
User packs are validated on load:
- Valid TOML syntax
- Required fields present
- Regex patterns compile
- No duplicate rule IDs (within pack)

## Testing
- Unit tests for pack loading
- Tests for inheritance resolution
- Tests for conflict handling
- Tests for invalid pack rejection

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] User packs load from ~/.config/wa/patterns/
- [ ] Pack format documented
- [ ] Inheritance (extends) works
- [ ] Conflict resolution is predictable
- [ ] Invalid packs rejected with clear errors
